p.strong: x86 TSO vs ARM Weak Ordering

g.text: mfence | Full fence | Prevents all reorderings
lfence | Load fence | Prevents Load-Load reorder
sfence | Store fence | Prevents Store-Store reorder
lock prefix | Atomic + full fence | lock cmpxchg
dmb sy | Full system | All memory ops complete
dmb ish | Inner shareable | Coherent with cores
dmb ishld | Load-load only | Acquire semantics
dmb ishst | Store-store only | Release semantics

div.p: Can Thread 2 observe y == 1 but x == 0 after Thread 1 writes x = 1; y = 1;?

g.text: ARMv8 ldar/stlr are more efficient than explicit dmb

div.p: TSO: Simple programming, fewer bugs. Optimized for single-thread performance with predictable multi-thread behavior.
Weak: Max hardware flexibility. Optimized for power efficiency. Allows aggressive store buffering.
✓ Load-Load: Preserved (In-Order) ✓ Store-Store: Preserved (In-Order) ✓ Load-Store: Preserved ✗ Store-Load: MAY REORDER
Thread A: store x ← 1 load r1 ← y // May execute BEFORE store
Store buffer holds writes (~50 cycles) Later loads can bypass buffered stores
✗ Load-Load: MAY REORDER ✗ Store-Store: MAY REORDER ✗ Load-Store: MAY REORDER ✗ Store-Load: MAY REORDER
Thread A: store x ← 1 store y ← 1 // May become visible BEFORE x Thread B: load r1 ← y // Sees y=1 load r2 ← x // But x=0!

div.h3: ⚠️ Use explicit memory ordering — never rely on x86 behavior!
